coredata.dc:description: Nanomaterials have been widely used in many fields, especially in biomedical and stem cell therapy. However, the potential risks associated with nanomaterials applications are also gradually increasing. Therefore, effective and robust toxicology models are critical to evaluate the developmental toxicity of nanomaterials. The development of stem cell research provides a new idea of developmental toxicology. Recently, many researchers actively investigated the effects of nanomaterials with different sizes and surface modifications on various stem cells (such as embryonic stem cells (ESCs), adult stem cells, etc.) to study the toxic effects and toxic mechanisms. In this review, we summarized the effects of nanomaterials on the proliferation and differentiation of ESCs, mesenchymal stem cells and neural stem cells. Moreover, we discussed the advantages of stem cells in nanotoxicology compared with other cell lines. Finally, combined with the latest research methods and new molecular mechanisms, we analyzed the application of stem cells in nanotoxicology.

head.ce:title: Advantages and prospects of stem cells in nanotoxicology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanomaterials have been widely used in many fields, especially in biomedical and stem cell therapy. However, the potential risks associated with nanomaterials applications are also gradually increasing. Therefore, effective and robust toxicology models are critical to evaluate the developmental toxicity of nanomaterials. The development of stem cell research provides a new idea of developmental toxicology. Recently, many researchers actively investigated the effects of nanomaterials with different sizes and surface modifications on various stem cells (such as embryonic stem cells (ESCs), adult stem cells, etc.) to study the toxic effects and toxic mechanisms. In this review, we summarized the effects of nanomaterials on the proliferation and differentiation of ESCs, mesenchymal stem cells and neural stem cells. Moreover, we discussed the advantages of stem cells in nanotoxicology compared with other cell lines. Finally, combined with the latest research methods and new molecular mechanisms, we analyzed the application of stem cells in nanotoxicology.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, with the widespread application of nanomaterials, the development of nanotoxicology has gained significant attention worldwide. Stem cells, which have self-renewal and differentiation potential, are ideal targets for in vitro developmental toxicology research. Stem cell toxicology models have also been widely used in nanotoxicology research. Embryonic stem cells (ESCs), mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs), neural stem cells (NSCs) and other stem cell lines have become the common cell lines to evaluate the toxicity of nanomaterials especially developmental toxicity.

In the current review, we reviewed the effects of nanomaterials on ESCs, MSCs and NSCs. We also compared the advantages and disadvantages of stem cells applied in nanotoxicology research. Finally, we give future perspectives on stem cells role in nanotoxicology research.

Nanomaterials have a small size, large specific surface area, and high surface chemical activity compared with other materials. With the rapid development of nanotechnology, nanomaterials are widely used in many fields, such as new building materials, better batteries, and clinical applications (Maynard, 2007). However, the potentially toxic effects of nanomaterials could not be ignored. The geometric size of nanomaterials is from 1 to 100 nm, which are larger than the biomolecules. Once the host ingests them, their biological effects may be different from other chemicals. Therefore, special attention should be paid to the toxic effect of nanoparticles. To prevent possible harm, it is critical to detect the biological safety of nanomaterials. Nanotoxicology emerges as a new field of toxicology research. Traditional toxicology pays more attention to the toxicity of foreign chemicals in the form of ions or molecules, which could not fully reflect the toxicity of nanoparticles. New models or methods are needed to analyze the impact of particulate matter on human health.

There have been concerns about the impact of particulates on human health for a long time. The World Health Organization has put forward regulations on the content and exposure time of suspended particles in the air in 1995. In 2003, Service et al. (Service, 2003) expressed concern about the possible toxic effects of nanomaterials. Donaldson et al. (2004) reported that the interaction between nanoparticles and cells, tissues or blood of organisms should be concerned, and the biological response of their target organs to nanoparticles should also be properly evaluated. Based on above mentioned problems, Oberdorster et al. (2005) formally proposed the subject of nanotoxicology, providing systematic opinions for the development of nanotoxicology.

In the last two decades, nanotoxicology has been well combined with the research results of traditional toxicology to analyze the toxicity of pollutants from the perspective of particles. The experimental methods and tools for nanotoxicology have been developed, which play a crucial role in understanding the toxicity of nanomaterials. The research methods for nanotoxicology are mainly performed through cell evaluation in vitro. With the development of cell biology and molecular biology, many novel technologies have been applied to nanotoxicology. For example, xCELLigence technology (Stefanowicz-Hajduk et al., 2016) could detect cell viability, morphological changes, and attachment of nanoparticles to cells. 3D microfluidics (Li et al., 2019) and 3D organoid scaffolds (Gu et al., 2020) have been gradually put into use when studying organ toxicity. Some computational methods, such as quantitative structure-activity relationship (QSAR) modeling (Buglak et al., 2019) and omic technology (Frohlich, 2017), also play important roles in the toxicity assessment of nanoparticles. With the help of these new technologies, nanotoxicology has made significant progress in the following aspects.

First, the understanding of the interaction between nanomaterials and organisms has been improved. Previous studies have shown that the uptake of nanoparticles by the host is related to the shape, size, concentration and surface modification of nanoparticles (Albanese et al., 2012). Cells usually ingest and expel nanoparticles by endocytosis and exocytosis (Rejman et al., 2004; Chithrani and Chan, 2007). When the nanomaterials contact the biological interface, many proteins coated on the nanoparticles may form a protein corona (Cedervall et al., 2007; Nel et al., 2009). A previous found that some nanomaterials with good biocompatibility after surface modification may play an important role in biomedicine (Tang et al., 2012). Next, nanotoxicology provided evidence for the distribution and transport of nanoparticles in vivo. It is found that the inhaled nanoparticles can be deposited in the lung (Oberdorster et al., 2005), liver (Jani et al., 1994), skin (Ryman-Rasmussen et al., 2006) and other tissues and organs. When the host inhales nanoparticles, it will be discharged by macrophages (Sell, 1987), ciliary movement (Jorissen, 1998) or lymph circulation (Dodson et al., 2000). Third, numerous studies are reported on the toxicity of different nanoparticles from the perspective of physical damage, chemical damage and other pathological damage. Wu et al.(Wu and Tang, 2018) reviewed the internal circulation of nanomaterials and their influence on different organs. They found that nanomaterials could cross the biological barrier and accumulate in different organs through blood circulation. Moreover, nanomaterials may cause lung toxicity, liver toxicity, spleen toxicity, kidney toxicity and brain toxicity through oxidative stress and immune response. At the gene level, nanomaterials might affect HMBG-1, IL-1 α, IL-33, thus inducing inflammation, leading to the sterile inflammatory reactions (Leso et al., 2018). With the increasing reports on the toxicity of nanomaterials, there are also many papers summarizing the environmental exposure risks of different nanomaterials. Hou et al. (2018) summarized the toxic effects of ZnO NPs on organisms and microorganisms. They found that the toxic effect of ZnO is related to its physical and chemical properties. The production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), the imbalance of zinc homeostasis caused by the release of Zn2+ and the damage to the integrity of cell membrane may be the reasons for the toxicity of ZnO NPs. There have been concerns about the biological and environmental risks of the most commercialized nanomaterial, such as AgNP. Souza et al. (2018) focused on the biosafety of AgNP at both micro and macro level. AgNP could enter cells at the micro-level and produce the "Trojan horse" effect, leading to cell apoptosis. From a macro view, the extensive use of AgNP results in ecological imbalance, especially the serious impact on the aquatic environment and organisms. All these studies provide a biological basis for the safe use of different nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Problems needed to be solved in nanotoxicology
ce:section.ce:para: Although great progress has been made in the development of nanotoxicology, there is still a great demand for the development of toxicology evaluation and high-throughput toxicity screening of nanomaterials.

First, nanoparticles could pass through the placental barrier and affect the development of fetus (Wick et al., 2010; Yamashita et al., 2011). Therefore, it is critical to have a suitable model to evaluate the developmental toxicity of nanomaterials. At present, many studies on developmental toxicity are performed through in vivo experiments, such as zebrafish or mice, which are time-consuming and expensive.

Many nanomaterials have been used in biomedical treatment. For example, silver nanoparticles with good antibacterial properties could be used as surgical instruments (Kim et al., 2007). In cell therapy, graphene (Menaa et al., 2015), magnetic nanomaterials (Himmelreich and Dresselaers, 2009), and some metal nanomaterials (Fathi-Achachelouei et al., 2019) are often used as carriers or probes for drug delivery (Wang et al., 2016a). Therefore, the biosafety of nanomaterials needs to be guaranteed, and there is a need for appropriate methods to screen the appropriate concentration of nanomaterials for biological therapy.
Meanwhile, with the increasing number of new nanomaterials, more efficient, robust, high-throughput methods are needed to evaluate their toxicity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The significance of stem cells in nanotoxicology

ce:section.ce:para: Stem Cells are a kind of cells with the ability of self-renewal and differentiation. According to the development stage, they could be divided into ESCs and adult stem cells.

When a fertilized egg divides and develops into a blastocyst, the inner cell mass cells are ESCs. ESCs have self-renewal ability, and could differentiate into all tissues in the body. Mouse embryonic stem cells (mESCs) were isolated in early 1981 (Evans and Kaufman, 1981), followed by human embryonic stem cells (hESCs) (Thomson et al., 1998), mouse (Takahashi and Yamanaka, 2006) and human (Takahashi et al., 2007) induced pluripotent stem cells. These important milestones have contributed tremendously to the application and development of stem cell research.

Many tissues and organs of adult animals, such as the epidermis and hematopoietic system, have the potential to regenerate. Adult stem cells play a key role in this process. Under certain conditions, adult stem cells either produce new stem cells or differentiate according to certain procedures to form new functional cells and maintain the dynamic balance of growth and decline of tissues and organs.

MSCs are a kind of adult stem cells that can self-renewal and differentiation (Pittenger et al., 1999), mainly for osteogenic, adipogenic and chondrogenic differentiation. Given the ethical problems and the limitation of materials, many methods of stem cell therapy rely on mesenchymal stem cells.
Other adult stem cells, such as neural stem cells, hematopoietic stem cells, etc., also play different roles at different stages of human development.

Compared with other cell lines, stem cells have advantages in toxicological research. First, it is difficult for some human cells to be directly derivation in primary culture, and they cannot be continuously cultured. Some immortalized cell lines, such as cancer cells, may accumulate many mutations. The toxicity results may not reflect the real toxic effects of pollutants in the human body. The emergence of stem cells can overcome these two problems. Meanwhile, the acquisition of induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) makes it possible to perform more individual toxicological research.

The rise of stem cell research also provides an effective means for the evaluation of nanomaterials. In 2005, it was reported that the study of cytotoxicity of nanomaterials had been conducted using mammalian germline stem cells (Vinardell, 2005). Although limited by experimental conditions and methods, the possibility of stem cells being applied in nanotoxicology research has been proposed for a long time. Stem cells have self-renewal and differentiation ability, which is an adequate model for developmental toxicity evaluation. There is also a long history of toxicological research with stem cells. In 1993, J. hueuer et al.(Heuer et al., 1993) used mESCs to study the developmental toxicity of teratogen repetitive acid in vitro. In 1995, ECVAM formally proposed the need for validated in vitro tests in reproductive toxicology (Brown et al., 1995). Next, establishing the EST method (Spielmann et al., 1997) provides a standard method for using ESCs for toxicological study. With the successful acquisition of hESCs (Thomson et al., 1998) and induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSCs) (Takahashi and Yamanaka, 2006; Takahashi et al., 2007), an increasing number of methods have been introduced into toxicology research. The research of stem cell-based nanotoxicology has attracted more attention, especially since 2014, numerous articles on the toxicology evaluation of stem cell-based nanomaterials have been published every year, and the research content has been gradually enriched. The development of stem cell toxicology (Faiola et al., 2015) will help us better understand the developmental toxicity of nanomaterials.

Moreover, as an in vitro toxicology research model, the stem cell toxicology model could quickly screen the toxicity of many new nanomaterials, to meet the needs of safety evaluation of rapidly growing nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advantage of stem cells compared with other cell lines in nanotoxicology study
ce:section.ce:para: Compared with other cell lines, stem cell lines are more commonly used to detect nanoparticles' developmental toxicity. Compared with other cell lines, stem cells have self-renewal and differentiation ability, making it possible to detect the effects of nanoparticles on all the tissues or organs of the human body.

First, some studies compared the ability of stem cells and other cell lines in up-taking nanoparticles. The results showed that stem cells could uptake more nanoparticles under the same conditions and show a serious impact. Compared with HeLa cells and fibroblasts, gold nanoparticles have a more accumulation in MSCs. After 50 μg/mL PEG HGNs exposure, the cell cycle of the three kinds of cells did not change significantly, but only the cell migration of MSC was seriously affected and found that the expression level of RUNX2, the key gene of osteogenic differentiation of MSC, increased significantly (Encabo-Berzosa et al., 2017). In another research on the toxic effects of gold nanoparticles, the differences, and connections between the effects of gold nanoparticles on neural stem cells, human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC cells) and PC12 cells were compared. Moreover, NSCs have the highest uptake of gold nanoparticles (Soenen et al., 2012).

At present, there are researches on the treatment of nanomaterials with the same time and concentration for stem cell lines, other adult cell lines or cancer cell lines. It was found that stem cell lines were more sensitive to the impact of nanomaterials, which showed the advantages of nanotoxicology used by stem cells. Jawad et al. found that 10 μg/mL TiO2 NPs would inhibit heart beating after 24h culture in human embryonic stem cell-derived cardiomyocytes (hESC-CM). However, at the same concentration, this effect is not reflected in fibroblast (Jawad et al., 2011). Another research compared the toxicity of AgNPs in human embryonic stem cell-derived fibroblasts and fibroblast cell line. In the cytotoxicity research, they found that hESCs derived fibroblasts were more fragile and sensitive than L-929 fibroblast cell line in the proliferation stage (Peng et al., 2012).

Compared with some cancer cell lines, a more sensitive effect compared with cancer cell lines on cell proliferation, cell viability and migration ability were found when treated with silicon carbide nanowires in hMSCs(Chen et al., 2018a). Among hESCs and lung cancer cells, hESCs showed a more sensitive effect in a short-term treatment (24h) (Keremidarska-Markova et al., 2018). Under the same exposure concentration and treatment time (no more than 20 μg/mL, 48 h treatment), cancer cell lines are more likely to induce apoptosis, while human mesenchymal stem cells (hMSCs) did not cause apoptosis and cause genotoxicity (Moratin et al., 2018). The environmental concentration of nanomaterials is very low. However, it is not clear whether environmental concentration nanomaterials could affect human health. The lower concentration of nanoparticles can lead to abnormal proliferation and differentiation of stem cells, suggesting that the toxicity of nanoparticles in environmental concentration could not be ignored.

In addition, the potential of multiple differentiation potential enables stem cells to be used in developmental toxicology research. Embryonic stem cells could differentiate into neural, heart, liver, and other organs, and to study the neurodevelopmental toxicity, cardiomyocyte developmental toxicity and hepatocyte developmental toxicity of nanomaterials. MSCs have a wide range of sources, are easy to obtain. Their adipogenesis and osteogenesis ability makes them widely used in the developmental toxicology of nanomaterials. Neural stem cells are also important materials for neurodevelopmental toxicity research. The above research works on developmental toxicity are difficult to be realized by other cell lines.

Although there are few researches on nanotoxicology with induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) (Handral et al., 2016; Yamada et al., 2018), the use of iPSC in toxicology reduces ethical problems and provides the possibility for personalized toxicology (Faiola et al., 2015). This is also the advantage of stem cell lines in nanotoxicology research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Present applications of stem cells in nanotoxicology
Effects of nanomaterials on different stem cells
ce:section.ce:para: ESCs have the self-renewal ability and could differentiation into almost all kinds of adult cells. Firstly, ESCs could differentiate into embryoid bodies, and the influence of pollutants on global differentiation could be detected in vitro. Then, ESCs could be differentiated into different germ layers to detect the influence of pollutants on a specific germ layer or organ developmental stage. The development of EST (embryonic stem cell test) (Brown et al., 1995; Spielmann et al., 1997) has laid a solid foundation for applying stem cells to detect the developmental toxicity of pollutants. A review summarizes the application of ESCs in the risk assessment of environmental pollutants. It is considered that the ESCs test is a promising research method to test the potential developmental toxicity of nanomaterials (Rezvanfar et al., 2016). We list the articles on evaluating toxic effects of nanomaterials by using ESCs in Table 1 and discuss them in detail. The results were summarized in Fig. 1 . First, we focus on the effect of nanomaterials on mESCs. When mESCs were first used for nanotoxicology detection, the effect of nanomaterials on cell viability was mainly concerned. Tran et al. studied the effect of different concentrations of mix-size polystyrene nanoparticles on the development of mESCs from the morphological point of view. More fibroblast-like cells were produced under the high concentration nanoparticles treatment, while more embryoid bodies occurred in low concentration treated groups (Tran et al., 2007). According to the EST experimental method, the cytotoxicity of different coated gold nanoparticles or cobalt ferrite to mESCs and the toxicity of cardiomyocyte differentiation were detected, and the embryotoxicity was evaluated and classified. The results showed that cobalt ferrite nanoparticles coated with gold were evaluated as non-embryotoxicity, and the cobalt ferrite nanoparticles coated with silanes (Si–CoFe) and gold nanoparticles coated with hyaluronic acid (HA–Au) exhibit weak embryotoxicity (Di Guglielmo et al., 2010). Subsequently, more studies focused on the genotoxic effects of nanomaterials on ESCs. The research of the DNA damage induced by multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWNTs) in mESCs reminds us to pay attention to the ROS-induced genotoxicity caused by nanoparticles (Zhu et al., 2007). The more severe DNA damage was found in the coated AgNPs than uncoated AgNPs in mESCs(Ahamed et al., 2008). These papers found the up-regulation of DNA damage-related genes, p53, Rad51 and phosphorylated-H2AX expression induced by different nanoparticles in mESCs. The self-renewal and differentiation ability are the basic characteristics of ESCs and the focus of toxicity research. First, the effect of nanomaterials on the proliferation of mESCs, Rajanahalli et al. (2015) found that different coated 20 nm AgNPs at 5 μg/mL could affect the cell cycle of mESCs, and then affect the proliferation of mESCs. Meanwhile, Oct4A related to the pluripotency decreased and Oct4B, which was related to cellular stress, increased. When considering the differentiation ability of mESCs, Park et al. studied the toxic effects of silica nanoparticles with EST method. They found that 10 nm and 30 nm silica nanoparticles would inhibit the differentiation of EBs into spontaneously contracting cardiomyocytes and showed embryotoxicity in a dose-dependent manner (Park et al., 2009). Another study found that monocarps (SWCNTs) and multilayers (MWCNTs) nanocarbon tubes could affect the efficiency of myocardial differentiation during the 7-day exposure period of mESCs (Imai et al., 2012). The effect of nanomaterials on the proliferation and differentiation of mESCs also worthy of investigation. After 24 h of exposure to 20 μg/mL of iron oxide particles, mESCs began to show a significant decline in cell viability. Moreover, this phenomenon may be caused by the production of ROS in cells (Rostami et al., 2015). Wu et al. studied the effect of layered double hydroxide nanoparticles (LDH NPs) on the proliferation and self-renewal of mESCs. Up to 40 μg/mL, LDH NPs treatment for at least 5 days would enhance the self-renewal ability of mESCs with or without LIF in morphology and RNA level. Further researches showed the activation of the PI3K/AKT signaling pathway upon LDH NPs treatment (Wu et al., 2015).

There are some differences between hESCs and mESCs; among them, mouse embryonic stem cells have higher pluripotency (Hanna et al., 2010). When testing the toxicity effects of pollutants, hESCs may more accurately reflect the impact of pollutants on the human body (Liu et al., 2017). In our experiment, we studied the effects of AgNP on the neural development of mouse ESCs and human ESCs, respectively. Although the conclusions are similar, we found that under the same concentration, AgNP could interfere with the neuron differentiation of mESCs(Yin et al., 2018); however, the effect on the cranial placode differentiation of hESCs was more significant (Hu et al., 2020). Some studies focused on the effect of nanomaterials on the proliferation and pluripotency of hESCs(Peng et al., 2012; Senut et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2018), while others use hESCs to further study the toxic effects of nanomaterials on human body. At present, the methods of neural differentiation of human embryonic stem cells were gradually mature, and many toxicity tests were also performed based on these neural differentiation models. The potential effects of many nanomaterials on neural development have also been revealed. Schwartz et al. (2015) gave a detailed method to detect the neurodevelopmental toxicity of pollutants by neural differentiation of hESCs. Many methods of neurodevelopmental toxicity test with nanomaterials have emerged. Hoelting et al. used a hESCs derived 3D model to test the developmental neurotoxicity of chemically inert polyethylene NPs (PE-NPs), and tested the acute and long-term toxicity. PE-NPs could penetrate the 3D neurosphere, thus causing cytotoxicity. However, after a long-term differentiation of about 18 days, some later progenitor marker genes were inhibited, suggesting the effect of PE-NPs on neuronal development and telencephalon differentiation (Hoelting et al., 2013). Another study investigates the effect of different coatings of AgNPs on hESCs derived glutamatergic neurons. It was found that 20 nm citrate-coated AgNPs would induce cell damage under low concentration (0.1 μg/mL). While, polyvinylpyrrolidone-coated AgNP (AgSPs) induced similar effects until 1 μg/mL. In this in vitro model, they also found that the change of phosphorylation status of GSK-3 a/b and Tau is common in Alzheimer's disease (Begum et al., 2016). Another article systematically summarizes the methods of neural differentiation of hESCs or induced pluripotent stem cells induced by AgNP(Hong et al., 2019). Hong et al. established a mature non-adherent glutamatergic neurons and astrocyte co-culture system through a 27-day differentiation process. After treatment with AgNP at a concentration of no more than 1.5 μg/mL, the developmental neurotoxicity of AgNP could be evaluated from cell viability, ROS, and morphology. Their research also showed that AgNP could induce ROS production, slow down neuritic growth, and increase the phosphorylation levels of GSK3β and Tau. Another hESCs-based neuron and astrocyte network model was established by Repar et al. to study the neurotoxicity of citrate-coated AgNP. They found that astrocytes were more sensitive to AgNP. After establishing the neural network, the number of astrocytes increased, but the morphology of neurons and astrocytes would be affected. Meanwhile, the activation of the AKT/GSK-3/caspase-3 pathway may cause neurodegeneration diseases (Repar et al., 2018). According to Senut et al., AuNPs influence the proliferation and neural differentiation of hESCs related to size. In addition to the most toxic 1.5 nm, 4 nm and 14 nm AuNPs did not affect the cell viability at no more than 10 μg/mL. However, AuNPs influences the epigenetic modification of hESCs. It was found that after 24 h of exposure, all sizes of 10 μg/mL AuNPs significantly reduced the level of 5 mC in cells (Senut et al., 2016). The development of omic technology also further elucidates the influence of nanoparticles on the development of hESCs. Using microarray analysis, 25 μg/mL of AgNP with a size of about 10 nm was found to affect NRF2 regulated oxidative stress signaling pathway or neurodevelopmental related pathway, and then affect the morphology or function of neural precursor cells induced by hESCs, thus producing developmental neurotoxicity (Oh et al., 2016). Another research with proteomics method showed that TiO2 could cause DNA damage, generate oxidative stress, and ultimately affect cell differentiation from the proteomic results (Pan et al., 2018). Based on the proteomic data, TiO2 showed a significant disturbance effect on mesodermal differentiation in the further process of mesodermal directional differentiation. In the above studies, the signal pathway may be different from the previous research due to the different concentrations of the nanoparticles. Nevertheless, the omic study provides a reasonable basis for the toxic mechanism of the effects of nanoparticles on ESCs. Therefore, the effects of nanoparticles on the proliferation and differentiation of ESCs, whether mESCs or hESCs, could be used as reasonable endpoints to evaluate the developmental toxicity and mechanism of nanoparticles.
(2)
Mesenchymal stem cells

The effects of different nanomaterials on the proliferation and differentiation of MSCs are listed in Table 2 . The results were summarized in Fig. 2 . Many experiments use MSCs to study the toxic effects of nanomaterials; therefore, we classified and discussed different types of materials in detail.

Firstly, the influence of carbon-based nanomaterials, such as graphene, quantum dots and carbon nanotubes, on MSCs is summarized in nonmetallic nanomaterials. Some carbon nanomaterials have good biocompatibility and are thus used as drug carriers. The following two main characteristics are presented in the research process: high exposure dose, different kinds and shapes of carbon nanomaterials have different effects on MSCs. Firstly, the effects of carbon-based nanomaterials on the proliferation of MSCs are summarized. Carbon nanoparticles showed a more serious effect after 72h treatment, even at a low concentration (25 μg/mL). Furthermore, a significant G0/G1 cell cycle arrest occurred after 50 μg/mL CNPs treatment for 48h in human MSCs(Periasamy et al., 2016a). A high dose (400 μg/mL) of carbon nanostructures (CNs) for at least 48h treatment induced significant cell necrotic death in human MSCs due to the acute oxidative stress. Meanwhile, when treated with at least 200 μg/mL CNs for at least 48h, the cell cycle changed and more cells were found in the e sub-G0 phase because of the alteration of CYP1A and P53 (Al-Hadi et al., 2016). Considering the differentiation of MSCs, Mooney et al. detected the distribution and influence of carbon nanotubes in human MSCs. They found that carbon nanotubes with a concentration of no more than 32 μg/mL had little effect on the proliferation and differentiation of MSCs. However, carbon nanotubes will cross the cell membrane and locate near the nucleus (Mooney et al., 2008). Up to 50 μg/mL graphene quantum dots (GQDs) promote osteogenic and adipogenic differentiation in RNA and protein level (Qiu et al., 2016). A biocompatible material, graphene/single-walled carbon nanotube (G/SWCNT) hybrids treatment at 5 μg/mL would promote osteogenic differentiation while inhibiting adipogenic differentiation for 7 days by activating the p38 signaling pathway (Yan et al., 2016). Liu et al. found that SWCNTs and carboxylated MWCNTs with a low concentration of 3 μg/mL could significantly affect the activity and proliferation of MSCs, and inhibit their osteogenic and adipogenic differentiation. Further study showed that the effect of these two materials on the SMAD-dependent bone morphogenetic protein (BMP) signaling pathway might be the key factor affecting the differentiation of MSCs(Liu et al., 2010b). Low-dose carbon black (Printex 90, 3 ng/mL and 30 ng/mL) could inhibit the osteogenic differentiation of MSCs by affecting the synthesis of mitochondria during the differentiation process. During osteogenic differentiation, PGC-1 α, Nrf1 and TFAM were significantly inhibited (Shen et al., 2018). In contrast, the toxic effect of graphene oxide is weak. Huang et al. examined the effect of polyethylene glycol - (PEG) xylated reduced graphene oxide (PRGO) with a maximum concentration of 500 μg/mL on MSCs, and found that PRGO did not affect the promotion and differentiation potential of mouse MSCs(Huang et al., 2017). However, graphene could promote the osteogenic differentiation of MSCs without affecting the proliferation of MSCs(Nayak et al., 2011).

Silica nanoparticles are also an important inorganic nanomaterial, and there are many reports about their influence on MSC. The influence of different shapes of silica nanoparticles is not the same. Silicon carbide nanoparticles (SiCNPs) and nanowires (SiCNWs) at 100 μg/mL showed different toxic effects to human MSCs because of the different cytocompatibility to hMSCs. SiCNWs showed toxic risk on the osteogenic and adipogenic differentiation. These phenomena may be caused by the significant upregulation of MCP-1 and IL-8. Then, in the silicon carbide nanowires treatment groups, hMSCs showed a more sensitive effect than the cancer cell lines on cell proliferation, cell viability and migration ability (Chen et al., 2018a). During the proliferation stage, a significant cell cycle disturbance was found after SiO2 treatment. Furthermore, hMSCs were found on the sub G0 and G1 cell circle phases after being treated by SiO2 nanoparticles (Periasamy et al., 2015). Two studies have found that this phenomenon may be caused by activating the ERK1/2 signaling pathway (Kim et al., 2015a; Chen et al., 2019). During the differentiation stage, synthetic silicate nanoplatelets could promote osteogenic differentiation of human MSCs at a higher concentration, i.e., 100 μg/mL (Gaharwar et al., 2013).

Considering the effect of metal nanoparticles on stem cells, we should first pay attention to the effect of SPIO on MSCs. Superparamagnetic iron oxide particles (SPIO) could be used for medical transfer. Different surface coatings of magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) impacted the aggregation status of the nanoparticles, but did not affect the degradation of MNPs in cells. In the long-term culture process, the biodegradation efficiency of hMSCs to particles is related to the initial exposure concentration, and the surface coating has little effect on this process (Plan Sangnier et al., 2019). Although there are different research directions, SPIO can generally affect the proliferation and differentiation of MSCs. When studying the effect on proliferation, we found that, the cell cycle of rat bone marrow derived MSCs was accelerated since more cells were in S phase after two novel amino acid (lysine or glycine)-coated superparamagnetic nanoparticles (SPIO) treatment at low concentration (1 μg/mL). In this way, the cell proliferation ability was enhanced (Yu et al., 2016). Meanwhile, they also found that more hMSCs would stay in the sub-G0/G1 phase when treatment with high concentrations (50 and 200 μg/mL) of Fe3O4 nanoparticles (50–75 nm) for 24 h. When focused on the differentiation process, long-term (7 weeks) treatment of superparamagnetic iron oxide particles (SPIO) on MSCs can cause SPIO deposition in cells and affect the adipogenic and chondrogenic differentiation (Farrell et al., 2008). Recent research with omic study showed that iron oxide nanoparticles at 100 μg/mL promoted osteogenic differentiation of human bone-derived MSCs by activating MAPK signaling pathway (Wang et al., 2016b).

Different coating, size and concentrations of gold nanoparticles have different effects on MSCs. Culture with 31.6 μg/mL and 5-nm AuNPs s would induce significant cell death within 5 days. While 15- and 30-nm GNPs had low cytotoxicity, but they could inhibit the osteogenic and adipogenic differentiation of human bone marrow MSCs(Fan et al., 2009). Another study found that up to 22.8 μg/mL AuNP (10 nm and 80 nm) would not affect human MSCs proliferation after 7 days of treatment. However, a higher concentration of 10 nm gold nanoparticles affected the mitochondrial activity after 7 days of exposure. AuNP inhibited the adipogenic differentiation stage under at least 11.6 μg/mL (Kohl et al., 2011). Meanwhile, Fan et al. found size-dependent toxic effects of AuNP on human bone marrow MSCs. Moreover, they found that small-sized AuNP has a more serious effect on hBMSCs self-renewal, while large-sized particles impact the osteogenic and adipogenic differentiation stage (Fan et al., 2012). The effects of the size and charge of AuNP on the proliferation and differentiation of MSCs were reported in two articles (Li et al., 2015, 2016). 0.5 nM AuNP–NH2 (+), AuNP–COOH (−), AuNP–OH (−) and AuNP-citrate (−) were added to the medium and cell viability was tested on the 1st, 3rd, and 7th day of proliferation. No obvious change was observed in the proliferation stage compared with the control group. It was found that MSCs more easily absorbed positive coated particles. Surface modifications have little effect on the differentiation, but AuNP–COOH could significantly reduce ALP activity and calcium deposition during osteogenic differentiation (Li et al., 2015). In addition to 40 nm, rod-shaped AuNP, other shapes and sizes would not affect the cell viability of MSCs after 3 days of treatment at a concentration of no more than 0.5 mM 50 μM AuNP with different sizes and shapes would not affect the proliferation of osteogenic cells in the 14 days treatment. However, the size and shape of AuNP would affect the ALP expression in the osteogenic differentiation stage (Li et al., 2016). Smaller gold nanoparticles (sub-10nm) have the opposite effect on adipogenic and osteogenic differentiation, and there are interspecific differences. For example, gold nanoparticles (sub-10 nm) could promote adipogenesis and inhibit osteogenesis. The results are just the opposite in mice. (Yi et al., 2010; Li et al., 2017).

The effect of AgNP on MSC is mainly based on its concentration and size. Up to 10 μg/mL 10 nm and 20 nm AgNPs had no effects on the human adipose-derived MSCs proliferation while slightly affecting the osteogenic and adipogenic differentiation. However, in their research, they only exposed AgNPs for 24 h before differentiation and did not go deep into the molecular level to detect the status of human adipose-derived MSC, and thus the experimental conclusions have some limitations (Samberg et al., 2012). The uptake of high concentration (over 20 μg/mL) of 80 nm AgNPs after 24h would affect cell viability. However, it could not be ignored that a low concentration of AgNPs (lower than 10 μg/mL) could affect the adipogenic and osteogenic differentiation of mesenchymal stem cells (Sengstock et al., 2014). In 2016, He and Hellmlinger et al. studied the effect of PVP-coated AgNP on the proliferation and differentiation of MSC. After 48h treatment, 12.5–100 μg/mL PVP coated AgNP induced G2/M arrest, and in a higher 200 μg/mL treatment group, both G2/M and S arrest occurred (He et al., 2016c). Meanwhile, 10 μg/mL PVP coated AgNP may promote the adipogenic differentiation of hMSCs(He et al., 2016b). However, there was no significant change in osteogenic differentiation (He et al., 2016a). However, two other studies have reached different conclusions. AgNPs may enhance the proliferation ability of MSCs (4 μM) and promote their osteogenic differentiation ability by activating TGF β signaling pathway (Qin et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2015).

The effect of TiO2 nanoparticles on MSC is the same. The results of microarray analysis showed that TiO2 with a larger size could promote the proliferation, differentiation, and expression of immune stress-related genes of MSCs. The upregulation of ATP1A2 and MAP3K11 genes was closely related to osteogenic differentiation (Yu et al., 2015). Lv et al. found that TiO2 could affect the differentiation of MSCs at the epigenetic level (Lv et al., 2015). However, TiO2 obtained by the green synthesis method would reduce the toxic effects on MSCs(Koca and Duman, 2018). In addition to SPIO, AgNP, AuNP and TiO2, the effects of other metal nanomaterials on MSCs are also summarized in Table 2.

In general, the effect of nanomaterials on the proliferation and differentiation (adipogenic and osteogenic differentiation) of MSCs has been widely studied. In non-metallic nanomaterials, different kinds of carbon nanoparticles have different effects on MSCs, while the researchers showed that silicon nanoparticles could activate the ERK1/2 signaling pathway and affect MSCs proliferation. SPIO could inhibit MSCs proliferation by affecting cell cycle in metal nanomaterials and promote osteogenic differentiation by activating the MAPK pathway. TiO2 may promote the proliferation and osteogenic differentiation of MSCs. Then, different sizes and concentrations of AuNP and AgNP have different effects on MSCs.
(3)
Neural stem cells

It is critical to study the neurodevelopmental toxicity of pollutants because they may have irreversible effects on human neurodevelopment through the placental barrier and blood-brain barrier. In addition to mesenchymal stem cells, the neural stem cell line is a widely used adult stem cell line in toxicology research. NSCs can proliferate and differentiate in vitro, further forming different types of neural cells such as neurons, astrocytes and oligodendrocytes, which could be used to detect the neurodevelopmental toxicity of pollutants (Liang et al., 2019b). We listed the influence of nanomaterials on NSCs in Table 3 , and discussed some key findings. The results were summarized in Fig. 3 .

Nanoparticles labeled NSCs would benefit the delivery of medicine and have a better targeting effect (Cao et al., 2017). Moreover, it may help in tracking of NSCs(Vande Velde et al., 2012). Although nanomaterials are good supplements for applying NSCs in biomedicine, their effects on the metabolism, gene modification, proliferation, and differentiation of NSCs cannot be ignored (Liu et al., 2018). An earlier study showed that NSC could ingest more nanoparticles than other cell lines. Therefore, the conditions and effects of NSC ingestion of nanoparticles should be considered first (Soenen et al., 2012). The ability of different coated AgNP to enter mouse NSCs was not the same. Pongrac et al. found that AgNP was mainly ingested by cells through macropinocytosis, but different surface coatings would have different effects on the uptake efficiency and toxicity of AgNP(Pongrac et al., 2018). However, Pavicic et al. studied the response of mouse NSCs to AgNP with different surface charges (neutral, positive, and negative). They found that different AgNP of 0.5 and 5 μg/mL could significantly affect the NSCs after 24 h of exposure from the perspectives of cytotoxicity, oxidative stress, gene expression and DNA damage. The effect of the concentration on the uptake of nanoparticles is greater than the surface coating (Pavicic et al., 2019). Meanwhile, the process and mechanism of the endocytosis and exocytosis of TiO2 by mouse NSCs were studied in detail. The size and shape of TiO2 affected the endocytosis process, while the exocytosis process was related to the concentration and shapes (Wang et al., 2013b). These studies show that NSCs can ingest nanomaterials, and the amount of ingestion depends on their surface modification, concentration, size and shapes.

The proliferation and differentiation of NSCs will be affected by the nanomaterials. In the proliferation, a study with rat NSCs showed that at least 2 μg/mL AgNP damaged the neuronal growth process (Guo et al., 2017). Wang et al. found that mouse NSCs could ingest TiO2 nanoparticles and Lysosomes first metabolized TiO2. The effect of TiO2 concentration higher than 150 μg/mL on NSCs was dose-dependent and time-dependent, and the low concentration of TiO2 might promote cell proliferation (Wang et al., 2013a).

During NSCs differentiation, nanomaterials may have different effects on the differentiation process of NSCs and different kinds of neural cells derived from NSCs. Park et al. (2011) found that the treatment group covered with graphene at the bottom of the culture dish induced more neurons after a long time (about a month) of human NSCs differentiation than the control group. A similar result was found by Akhavan et al.(Akhavan and Ghaderi, 2013), and the promotion of neurons and reduction of astrocytes induced by NSCs was found after being treated with graphene/TiO2. A mouse NSCs-based study simulated the uptake of nanoparticles by the central nervous system in early embryonic development. NSCs were differentiated into three kinds of cells, including neurons, astrocytes, and oligodendrocytes, which constituted the central nervous system. After the exposure of fluorescent transfection-grade magnetic particle (MP) (about 160 nm), it was found that astrocytes could take in more particles due to their high surface activity (Fernandes and Chari, 2014). The surface modification of nanomaterials may also affect its toxic effect during differentiation. Different surface modifications (plain SiO2, SiO2_NH2, SiO2_SH or SiO2_PVP) have different surface affinities for different kinds of cells formed by NSCs differentiation and may accumulate in different cells. Plain SiO2, SiO2_NH2 or SiO2_SH would accumulate in the astrocytes and microglial cells, while SiO2_PVP showed the lowest cellular uptake level (Izak-Nau et al., 2014).

Finally, some studies compared the effects of nanomaterials on human NSCs and mouse NSCs, and found that human NSCs may be more suitable for studying the toxic effects of nanomaterials. After 1, 5, 10, and 20 μg/mL AgNP treatment for 24 h, human and mouse NSCs cell viability was affected by AgNPs treatment. AgNP could affect the proliferation ability of human and mouse NSCs by affecting the cell cycle. Lower doses showed toxicity, reflecting the higher sensitivity of human NSCs(Liu et al., 2015). Joris et al. used six kinds of human or mouse NSCs from different sources to detect the neurotoxicity of DMSA-coated iron oxide nanoparticles (IONPs). The opposite ROS trend and calcium levels in mouse NSCs than other cell lines suggested a different nanotoxicology between species (Joris et al., 2017). Based on the above conclusions, due to interspecific differences, to reflect the impact of nanomaterials on human neural development more accurately, human NSCs rather than mouse NSCs should be used as research materials.

Based on the above studies, it could be found that nanoparticles could be absorbed by NSCs in large quantities and affect the proliferation of NSCs. In NSCs differentiation process, it was found that NSCs induced astrocytes were more sensitive to the treatment of nanoparticles. More importantly, human NSCs might be more suitable for nanotoxicology research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the toxic mechanism of nanoparticles on stem cells
ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, there has been a higher demand and requirement for studying the developmental toxicity mechanism of nanomaterials. The use of stem cells can also help us better understand the developmental toxicity mechanism and molecular events in the nanomaterials from the perspective of organelles or molecules. The omic technology has also been widely used in toxicology mechanism research, including genomics and proteomics. The first transcriptomics-based EST (Embryonic stem-cell test) method published by van Dartel et al. (van Dartel et al., 2010) provided a more in-depth analysis method for the study of the toxicity mechanism of nanomaterials. The mechanisms were summarized in Fig. 4 .

Oxidative stress has always been the focus of nanotoxicology research. In the past, the oxidative stress damage caused by nanomaterials was evaluated by detecting ROS production (Deng et al., 2009; Hackenberg et al., 2011; Syama et al., 2014). Mitochondria are also the main targets of ROS. At present, some studies have analyzed the toxic mechanism of nanomaterials from the perspective of mitochondrial damage and its molecular mechanism. A study with rat NSCs showed that at least 2 μg/mL AgNP damaged the neuronal growth process. From the oxidative stress level and RNA level, they found that the damage was related to the effect of mitochondrial-related pathways (Guo et al., 2017). Shen et al. found that low dose carbon black (Printex 90, 3 ng/mL and 30 ng/mL) inhibits the osteogenic differentiation of MSCs by affecting the synthesis of mitochondria during the differentiation process. During osteogenic differentiation, PGC-1 α, NRF1 and TFAM were significantly inhibited (Shen et al., 2018). Another research found that the cells treated with up to 0.3 μg/mL AgNP could significantly reduce the expression level of MFN1, which made the mitochondrion show fission and fusion (Yamada et al., 2018). According to a toxicogenomic study with the microarray method, after 24 h treatment, 5 μg/mL AgNP would induce 101 DEGs in the spontaneous differentiation stage of mouse ESCs. Most cellular responses were similar to Ag ions, such as embryonic development and metabolism-related genes. More genes were changed in the Ag+ treated groups. However, some oxidative stress-related genes were only changed in AgNP treated groups (Gao et al., 2017). The results showed that the oxidative stress caused by nanomaterials might come from particles rather than ions.

In addition to the above molecular mechanisms to explain the developmental toxicity of nanomaterials, recent studies have also paid attention to the effects of nanomaterials on cells of different gender, to understand the toxic mechanism of nanomaterials. Serpooshan et al. found that the effect of nanoparticle intake was different in different sex-derived cells. Female's amniotic stem cells could absorb more nanoparticles (take quantum dots as an example), but in the salivary gland primary fibroblasts, the particles are more concentrated in the male cells (Serpooshan et al., 2018). In another study on the toxicity of AgNP using female mouse embryonic stem cells, low dose (below 1 μg/mL) of AgNP may lead to X-chromosomal inactivation (XCI), which has a significant impact on the differentiation and development process (Zhang et al., 2019a). These studies suggest that cell sex is also an important factor in nanotoxicology.

Finally, nanoparticles may affect the differentiation process of stem cells through an epigenetic mechanism, but this research is still relatively naive. When studying the toxic mechanism of TiO2 on the osteogenic differentiation of hMSCs, Lv et al. (2015) found that the upregulate methylation level of histone H3 at lysine 4 (H3K4) might be the possible reason for the promotion effect. Moreover, Liu et al. (2016) found that single-layer graphene may activate osteogenesis by H3 at lysine 4 (H3K4) methylation. These studies have proved that some nanomaterials may change the chromosome conformation by entering the nucleus and eventually affecting stem cell differentiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The prospect of stem cells in nanotoxicology
Advanced research technology for current stem cell research
ce:section.ce:para: At present, stem cell research has been greatly developed. The innovation of its culture method and technology makes the application of stem cells more extensive and more in-depth research. For example, the establishment of haploid ESCs (Yang et al., 2012; Li et al., 2018) may help further track a gene's long-term effect on animals. However, the establishment of extended pluripotent stem cells (EPS cells) (Yang et al., 2017) with the in vivo embryonic and extraembryonic potency provides a promising model for the study of early embryonic development in animals. In recent years, the development of 3D organoids culture technology (Lancaster and Knoblich, 2014) has also solved many problems of 2D culture, giving a deeper understanding of stem cell development.

Furthermore, many advanced biotechnologies have been applied to stem cell research, such as 3D printing technology, which further improves the biological safety of stem cells. The use of CRISPR/Cas9 technology and single-cell sequencing technology improves the gene function in stem cells. Although these methods have not been used in nanotoxicology, to understand the toxic effect of nanoparticles more quickly, deeply, and comprehensively in the future, these are indispensable technical means.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Disadvantages of stem cells in nanotoxicology
ce:section.ce:para: Although stem cells play an important role in nanotoxicology study, they still face some challenges. First, compared with in vivo experiments, in vitro research has been much cheaper. However, the price of stem cell research is still relatively expensive, limiting its large-scale use in nanotoxicology. Second, another major problem is that the current stem cell toxicology system cannot reflect the influence of nanomaterials on the whole organ. At present, organoid and organ-on-chip technology are expected to overcome this problem. Using the microfluidic system to connect organs together, which may simulate the relationship between organs. It can also more truly reflect the influence of nanomaterials on organs. Third, the promotion of proliferation and differentiation is often not considered a toxic effect in some current studies. If it leads to unlimited proliferation of stem cells in stem cell therapy, it will greatly impact the human body.

Some of the problems that need to be solved in nanotoxicology cannot be comprehensively studied by stem cells need to be analyzed in combination with the results of occupational exposure studies or epidemiological studies. Secondly, the study of some atmospheric nanoparticles with complex components belongs to the field of nanotoxicology, whose impact on embryo development and human health is very complex. Therefore, a more comprehensive evaluation method is needed.

Although the application of stem cells in nanotoxicology research still faces the above challenges, this experimental system has been rapidly developing. The application of stem cells in nanotoxicology shows the following trends: First, the method changes. In the past, the cells were pretreated for only a few hours, which has now become the treatment of pollutants in the whole differentiation process, making the experimental results more reasonable and representative. Second, the treatment dose has gradually decreased. These changes make the experimental results more reflect the real impact of pollutants on the human body. Third, an increasing number of experiments have begun to pay attention to the screening of the concentration of nanoparticles with biomedical value, especially for stem cell therapy. Finally, more transcriptomics-based studies should not only focus on cytotoxicity but also comprehensively analyze the toxicity of nanoparticles from gene and protein levels. In the future, how to break through the bottleneck mentioned above, establish a more rapid, economic, and stable nanotoxicology evaluation and prediction system based on stem cells, and make a deeper understanding of the toxic effect of particulates, needs to be paid attention.

ZW collected and collated relevant research results. SX collected and collated relevant research results. BW consulted the literature and was a major contributor in writing the manuscript. All authors read and approved the final manuscript.
